purpose: Draft (inactive) question objects derived from the Vinci 2026-07-08 quiz-3 batch -- a completed, handwritten 'Review Quiz #3' (Water Survival, SHLSOS Course #549), 14 fill-in-the-blank questions across 3 pages. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted.
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified'. q-quiz3-lifeboat4-location-scenario and q-quiz3-stationbill-itemslist are recommended needs-review (not quiz-derived-study) -- see each question's sourceReference for why.
duplicateCheckNotes: Cross-referenced against all 228 live questions in src/content/course.json before drafting -- see the fuller duplicateCheckNotes in vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json for the specific matches found. Four of the 14 source questions were deliberately not drafted at all (exact/near-exact duplicates of live content, no new angle): Q1 (bucket as sea-anchor substitute, duplicates q-quiz1a-seaanchor-substitute), Q3 (24-hour water/food rule, duplicates the premise of q-quiz1a-first-water-liferaft), Q10 (3 lifeboatmen for 50 persons, duplicates q-vinci-coi-lifeboatmen-4160's 41-60 band), Q13 (lifeboats lowered every 3 months, duplicates q-cfr-lifeboat-lowered-quarterly word-for-word). Q4's core hatchet-count/location fact also duplicates live q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet and was not redrafted; only its new 'near the releasing gear' placement detail was drafted as a distinct question.

questions.prompt: What is the standard requirement used to determine the length of a lifeboat's sea painter?

choices.text: At least twice the distance between the boat deck and the ship's waterline when the ship is light
A fixed length of 50 feet, regardless of the ship's size
choices.misconception: Sea painter length scales with the ship's freeboard, not a single fixed figure for every vessel.

choices.text: Exactly equal to the distance between the boat deck and the waterline, with no multiplier
choices.misconception: Understates the required length -- the actual requirement is at least twice that distance, not a 1-to-1 match.

choices.text: Half the distance between the boat deck and the waterline
choices.misconception: Inverts the actual ratio -- the painter must be longer than the deck-to-waterline distance, not shorter.

hints: Think about needing enough slack for the boat to reach the water safely even as the ship's freeboard changes with loading.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's sea painter must be at least twice as long as the distance between the boat deck and the ship's waterline when the ship is light (i.e., at its lightest draft) -- made of manila fiber or an equal-strength synthetic line at least 2 3/4 inches in circumference.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-1.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 2 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's two hatchets are secured by lanyards to each end of the boat. Where specifically, at each end, are they positioned?

choices.text: Near the releasing gear
Inside the sealed equipment locker
choices.misconception: Hatchets must be readily available for use, not sealed away in a locker.

choices.text: Underneath the side benches
choices.misconception: Confuses hatchet stowage with where buoyancy units are typically located.

choices.text: Wherever there's convenient space -- no specific spot is required
choices.misconception: A specific position near the releasing gear is required, not an arbitrary convenient spot.

hints: Think about why a hatchet needs to be close at hand to the gear that could become fouled or need emergency cutting.
questions.explanation: A lifeboat's two hatchets are secured by lanyards to each end of the boat, near the releasing gear -- positioned there so they're immediately at hand if gear becomes entangled or fouled and needs to be cut free.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-1.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 4 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. Note: the underlying 'one hatchet at each end' fact is already live via q-quiz1a-bow-stern-hatchet (also independently confirmed by 46 CFR 169.529(k)) -- this question deliberately tests only the additional 'near the releasing gear' placement detail, which is new content not covered by that existing question.

questions.prompt: What piece of lifeboat equipment reduces the possibility of the boat capsizing?

choices.text: A sea anchor (drogue)
The boat's compass
choices.misconception: A compass aids navigation, not stability.

choices.text: The bailer
choices.misconception: A bailer removes water that's already inside the boat -- it doesn't prevent capsizing.

choices.text: The releasing gear
choices.misconception: Releasing gear frees the boat from the falls during launch -- unrelated to stability once underway.

hints: Think about what keeps the boat's bow pointed into oncoming seas rather than turning broadside to them.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor (drogue) is lifeboat equipment that reduces the possibility of capsizing, primarily by holding the boat's bow into the seas and slowing unwanted drift.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 5 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: A lifeboat's sea anchor rig includes both a tripping line and a drag (holding) line. What's the difference in what each one does?

choices.text: The tripping line brings the sea anchor's apex forward and empties it of water so it can be retrieved; the drag line is what fills with water and creates drag while the sea anchor is deployed
Both lines do the same thing -- create drag -- just attached at different points
choices.misconception: Collapses two distinct functions -- retrieval/collapsing vs. active drag-creation -- into one.

choices.text: The drag line is used to retrieve and collapse the sea anchor; the tripping line creates the drag while deployed
choices.misconception: Reverses the two lines' actual roles.

choices.text: The tripping line and drag line are two names for the same single line
choices.misconception: Treats two distinct, separately-purposed lines as if they were one.

hints: One of these lines is for bringing the sea anchor IN; the other is for keeping it working WHILE it's out.
To get the most drag from a sea anchor, the line that creates that drag should be towed while slack is paid out.
questions.explanation: A sea anchor's tripping line brings its apex forward, emptying it of water so it can be pulled aboard -- it's the retrieval line. The drag (holding) line is the one that fills the sea anchor's cone with water and creates drag on the lifeboat while it's deployed; towing it with slack paid out creates the most drag.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, questions 6 and 9 of 14, combined -- question 6 asked only for the retrieval procedure, question 9 asked for both lines and their uses; merged into one compare-and-contrast question rather than drafted as two overlapping questions on the same tripping-line fact); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Your vessel has 3 lifeboats on each side, numbered by standard convention. Where is #4 lifeboat located relative to the other boats on its side?

choices.text: On the port side, aft of #2 and forward of #6
On the starboard side, aft of #2 and forward of #6
choices.misconception: Even-numbered lifeboats are on the port side by convention, not starboard.

choices.text: On the port side, forward of #2
choices.misconception: Reverses the fore-to-aft ascending order -- #2 is the forward-most boat on the port side, not #4.

choices.text: It could be on either side, depending on the vessel
choices.misconception: Lifeboat numbering follows a standardized, not vessel-specific, convention.

hints: Even numbers are on the port side, ascending from forward to aft -- so with 3 boats per side numbered #2, #4, #6, where does #4 fall?
questions.explanation: By standard convention, port-side lifeboats are even-numbered, ascending from forward to aft. With 3 boats per side (#2, #4, #6), #4 sits on the port side, aft of #2 and forward of #6.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 8 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. CONFIDENCE NOTE: the student's answer clearly states 'portside of the vessel' and 'forward of lifeboat #6'; the 'aft of #2' portion was reconstructed from the already-live standard numbering convention (q-ch2-numbering-official-vh) rather than directly read from the handwriting, since the connecting word linking to the inserted 'lifeboat #2' note was not clearly legible. Recommended needs-review rather than quiz-derived-study for this reason, even though it's a worked-example application of an already-confirmed live rule rather than a new fact.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is NOT typically something a ship's Station Bill lists?

choices.text: Cargo manifest and stowage plan
Emergency alarms, signals, and instructions
Crew members' emergency duties
The fire alarm signal

hints: A Station Bill is about emergency preparedness and duties, not cargo logistics.
questions.explanation: A Station Bill lists emergency-related items -- alarms, signals, and instructions; individual crew members' emergency duties; and the specific alarm signals used (e.g., the fire alarm). A cargo manifest and stowage plan is a separate document, unrelated to emergency muster/duty assignments.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-2.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 7 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. Note: the live q-ch1-vocab-stationbill-easy already tests the bare definition of a Station Bill; this question tests the same general fact from an enumerated-list/elimination angle using this student's own itemization (emergency alarms/signals/instructions, fire alarm, emergency duties, alarm signals). Given the substantial overlap with already-live content and the low amount of genuinely new information, recommended needs-review / low promotion priority rather than a front-line status, per the same reasoning already applied to other review-variant content in this project (e.g. q-vinci-limber-holes).

questions.prompt: What is the diameter requirement for the sheaves used with davit falls?

choices.text: The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least 12 times the diameter of the wire rope
The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least equal to the diameter of the wire rope
choices.misconception: Understates the required ratio -- a 1-to-1 match would be far too small to protect the wire rope from excessive bending stress.

choices.text: The diameter at the base of the groove must be at least 50 times the diameter of the wire rope
choices.misconception: Overstates the required ratio well beyond the actual requirement.

choices.text: There is no specific diameter requirement -- any sheave that fits the wire rope is acceptable
choices.misconception: A specific minimum ratio is required, not an arbitrary fit.

hints: Think about protecting the wire rope from excessive bending stress and fatigue as it runs over the sheave.
questions.explanation: A davit fall sheave's groove-base diameter must be at least 12 times the diameter of the wire rope running over it -- too small a sheave would over-stress and fatigue the wire.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 11 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: Why is it important to adequately lubricate a lifeboat davit's fall wires?

choices.text: Without lubrication, the wires will corrode internally and lose their flexibility
Lubrication is purely cosmetic and has no protective function
choices.misconception: Dismisses a genuine protective function -- lubrication actively prevents internal corrosion.

choices.text: Lubrication makes the wire rope heavier, which improves lowering speed
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated, incorrect benefit -- lubrication is about corrosion protection, not weight.

choices.text: It's only necessary in freshwater ports, not at sea
choices.misconception: Invents an unstated freshwater-specific exception -- the corrosion risk applies generally.

hints: Think about what happens to the individual wires bundled inside a wire rope if moisture reaches them and isn't kept out.
questions.explanation: Fall wires must be adequately lubricated because, without it, they'll corrode internally and lose their flexibility -- lubrication protects against that corrosion.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 12 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json

questions.prompt: When a lifeboat's davit is fitted with a winch, what material and construction are the falls typically made from?

choices.text: Wire rope, 6x19 regular lay, filler wire construction
Manila fiber rope, matching the sea painter's material
choices.misconception: Confuses the falls (which must withstand the mechanical load of a winch) with the sea painter's fiber-rope material.

choices.text: Nylon webbing, for maximum stretch under load
choices.misconception: Excess stretch under load is undesirable for falls -- wire rope is used specifically because it doesn't stretch significantly.

choices.text: Chain, for maximum strength
choices.misconception: Not the actual material used -- chain would be impractical to spool on a winch drum.

hints: Think about what needs to wind cleanly and repeatedly around a winch drum without excessive stretch.
questions.explanation: When a davit has a winch, the falls are made of wire rope -- specifically 6x19 regular lay, filler wire construction.
questions.sourceReference: Vinci source batch 2026-07-08-quiz-3, image 2026-07-07_064497-3.jpg (Review Quiz #3, question 14 of 14); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-08-quiz-3-source-intake.json. CONFIDENCE NOTE: '6x19 regular lay filler wire' is a real, standard wire-rope construction term consistent with the handwriting and used with reasonable confidence, but was not independently cross-verified against a second source the way some other batches' facts have been -- worth a quick confirmation before treating as fully settled.
